California

Redevelopment Agency
P. O. Box 1834
Sacranento, CA 9 5812 -18 3 4
Re: Your Reguest for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-91-4 66
Dear llr. Phillips:
This is in response to your Letter requesting assistance on
behatf of Sacramento city councilneDber Heather Fargo concerning
her duties under the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the
PoIitical Reform Act (the uAct").1 You are seeking additional
advice with respect to the proposed North sacramento RedeveJ.opment
Area. (See, Farqo Advice Letter, No. I-91-089.) You have
requesteE-T"r"tr"f assistance. 2
Please note that our advice is linited to the Politica}
Reform Act. The coDmission does not advise on other areas of the
Iaw pertaining to redevelopnent. In addition, this Ietter is
based on the facts presented to us. The connission does not act
as the finder of fact in rendering advice. (rn re oqelsbv (1975)
1 FPPC ops. 71. )
OUESTIONS
1. May Councilmenber Fargo participate in the adoption of a
proposed redevelopment plan for the North Sacranento Redevelop:nent
irrei, including tire selEction of boundaries, approvaL of a project

3. will the decisions on the North sacramento Redeveloprnent

4. May Councilmernber Fargo participate in Sacramento city
council decisions on redistricting?
CONCLUS IONS
1. Councilnember Fargo nay not participate in the adoption
of a proposed redevelopment plan for the North Sacramento
Redev;lobment Area, the selection of boundaries, approval of a
project irea conroittee, the designation of land uses within the
ir"i, 
o. the approval of the preliroinary Plan for-the project if
she or her spouse ohrns property within the redevelopnent area'
2. Councilrnenber Fargo raay participate in future decisions
concerning implementation of the various projects within the North
Sacraroent5 Redevelop:nent Area provided the future decisions are
not interrelated to the decisions for vhich the councilmember was
disqualified, and the councilneDber has no other conflicts of
intdrest with respect to the decision'
3. The 'tpublic generallyrt exception wilI not permit.
Councilrnenber Fargo to participate unless the decisions will
affect a significint 
segment of Sacrarnento in the same nanner as
the decisiois wiII affeit the interests of the councilmernber.
Assuming that all the landowners and residents in the North
sacraneito Redevelopnent Area wiII be affected in a substantially
"irit.t 
nanner by the decisions, the percentage of the population
oi ii"rur"nto 
in the North sacra,ento Redevelopnent Area. is too
"r"ii 
io be considered a significant seg"ment of the public
generally.
4.DecisionsconcerningredistrictinginSacramentowillnot
foreseeably affect the councilnember,s interests. 
Consequently,
councilneroLer Fargo is not Precluded fron - Participating . in the

d;ci=i"ns due to the econornic interests discussed in
this letter.
FACTS
CouncilurernberFargoisthesacranentocitycouncilnenberfor
District One of the ci€y. The Sacramento City Council-will be
.""=ia.ii"q 
the adoption of a proposed redeveloprnent plan. for the
North sacrinento RedevelopDent Area. The decisions will include
the selection of boundariis, approval of a project area connittee'

-466
Page 3
the designation of land uses rrithin the area, and the approval of
the prelininary plan for the project. The City is also
considering a iedistricting plan which rrould include a portion of
the propos6d redevelopment area in Councilmember Fargo's district'
The proposed boundaries of the North sacramento project area
include approximately 1,200 acres out of approximately 5,000 acres
which werl- in the redevelopment survey area. The proposed
boundaries were selected because they encourpass the nost blighted
areas in the survey area. The proposed project area consists of
36 percent industrial-zoned property and 28 percent property zoned
for'comrnercial and office uses. You estirnated that approxinately
1,700 different persons own property within the proPosed project
ii"u. 
Some of pioperty olrners in the area own rnore than a single
parcel.
Councilrnember Fargo's spouse is acguiring real property for
his business which is located in the redevelopnent survey area and
proposed project area. The property is currently zoned for
i"alstriai uies and is one-half acre in size. The councilmember's
spouse plans to rehabilitate the ProPerty for his business
consistent \.rith current zoning.

Thus, the councilrnernber has an indirect ownership interest in
her spouse's business. If the councilmember's spouse acquires the
propeity in question, even in the narne of his business, she will
Le deened to own a pro-rata share of the real property. 
(Section
82033.) Thus, for exarnple, if the counciLnember's spouse is the
sole owner of his business, he is deemed to wholly own the
property in question. If he is a 50 percent Partner, he is deerned
Lo own so peicent of the real proPerty. councilmember Fargo will

Foreseeabi L itv
whether the financial effects of a decision are reasonablv
foreseeabl,e at the tine a governmental decision is made depends on
th" facts;f 
each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseelble if there is a substantial Iikelihood that

is only a nere possibility, 
it is not reasonably foreseeabJ'e.
(Doranev Cares v. Downev Cornmunitv Development Commission (1987)
i9e ca1.epp.3d 938i rn re Thorner (1975) I FPPC ops. 198-)
As we discusseCl in the Farqo Advice Letter, the anticipated
result of any redevelopnent plan is to increase the proPerty
values and iirprove the business clirnate within the project. area'
The very natuie of redeveloprnent projects has led the Commission
to find- that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a
financial effect on real property values and business interests
located within or near project areas. (In re oqelsbv (1975) 1
FPPC ops. 71; Arme]]tg Advice Letter, No. A-90-499. ) If the
councilne:nber has an interest in real property and a business
located in the redevelopnent area, we presume that a financial
effect on these interests is foreseeable.

The Conmissj.on has adopted a series of regulations which provide
guidance concerning rhether the foreseeable financial effects of a
decision are naterial. 
(Regulation L8?02. ) These regulations
apply different standards depending on rrhether the decision wiII
aiie-tfy or indirectty affect the official's 
econornic interest.
As lre described in the $59 Advice Letter, the nateriality
of the financial effect depends on rrrhether the economic interest
is directly involved in the decision or indirectly involved.
(Regulation L8702.) If the decision directly _ affects . the
offlcial's real property interests, disgualif ication is required
unless the decision lri11 have no financial effect on the real
property interest. 
(Regnrlation 18?02.1.) An official's real
iroierti interest is diiectly involved in a redevelopnent decision
if:
The decision is to designate the survey area,
to select the project area, to adopt the
prelininary plin, to forn a project area connittee,
Lo certify the environnental docunent, to adopt the
redevelopnent p1an, to add territory to the
redevelopnent area, or to rescind or anend any of
the abovL decisionsr' and real Property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redeveloproent area.
Regulation L8702.1(a) (3) (D).
Thus, if the decision before the agency is to designate the
survey area, to select the project area, to adopt the prelininary
plan, to fom a project area conmittee, to certify the
Lnvironmental doiunent, to adopt the redevelopment Plan, to add
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or arnend any of

the above decisions, the councilmember must disqualify herself

official's 
economic interests, provided the decisions are not too

For example, if the resolution of a Later

Thus, where decisions concern discrete projects in the
redevelopnent area, the decisions must be analyzed independently
to deteririne if the councilnenber may still be required to
disqualify herself from the decisions.
According to negulation 18702.1(a) (3), the financial effect
of a decision on an official's real property is naterial and
disqualif ication required if the decision involves:
(1) The zoning or rezoning, annexation or deannexation,
sale, 'purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or excLusion frorn any
city, Lounty, district or other loca1 governnental subdivision, of
the- official,s real property (Regulation La702- 1(a) (3) (A) );
(2') The decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation
of a iicense, perroit or other land use entitle:nent authorizing a
specific use'oi uses of the official's real proPerty (Regulation
:-8702.1(a) (3) (B) ); or,
(3) The decision involves the irnposition, repeal or
rnoairicition of any taxes or fees assessed or inposed on the
official,s real property (Requlation 18702.1(a) (3) (c));
Moreover, rrhere a decision does not fall' into one of the
categories set forth above, the councilnember's real property nay
stili le indirecttv rnaterially affected and therefore require
aisquatif ication. The indirect effect of a decision on an
official's real property interest is naterial if:
(1) The real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of that real property,
is located within a 300 foot radius of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of tle decision,
irntlss Lhe decision wiII have no financial effect
upon the official's real proPerty interest.

improvernents to, streets, -lrater, seh'er, storD

properfy in vhich the official has an interest will

feet and any part of the real proPerty is located

the proposed boundaries) of the proPerty which is
